elements.sent-2411-001: For the first time, we build a generalization of the $U(n)$ Yang-Mills theory obtained by abandoning the condition of covariant constancy of the Hermitian form in the fibers: $\nabla_a g_{\alpha\beta' \ne 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-002: So this theory is a simpler analogue of the well-known metric-affine gravity with $\nabla_a g_{bc \ne 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-003: In our case the connection $\nabla_a$ and the Hermitian form $g_{\alpha\beta'}$ are two independent variables, and the total curvature and the total potential are no longer anti-Hermitian matrices: in addition to the usual $\bm{F _{ab}$ and $\bm{A _a$ they also obtain new Hermitian parts $\bm{G _{ab}$ and $\bm{B _a$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-004: It is shown that the Hermitian form $g_{\alpha\beta'}$ is a Higgs field breaking the general $GL(n,\mathbb{C )$ gauge symmetry to $U(n)$, and its perturbations are Goldstone bosons which can be eliminated from the theory by redefining other fields.}
elements.sent-2411-005: The result is a theory consisting of two non-trivially interacting gauge fields, one of which can be made massive while the other remains massless.
elements.sent-2411-006: Letting the mass of the second gauge field tend to infinity allows one to restore the usual YM.
elements.sent-2411-007: The deep kinship between the Yang-Mills theory (YM), which describes strong and electro-weak interactions, and the Einstein theory of gravity (EG) is commonplace today.
elements.sent-2411-008: This kinship is based on the fact that at the center of the formalism of both theories is the same geometric object---the covariant derivative or connection.
elements.sent-2411-009: In case of YM, it has the form \begin{equation}\label{CovDerYM} \nabla_a \bm{\varphi} = (\partial_a - ie\bm{A}_a) \bm{\varphi},\end{equation} where $\partial_a$ is the coordinate derivative, $\bm{\varphi \cong \varphi^\alpha$ is the gauge-charged field, $\alpha$ is the internal color index\footnote{If one expression is obtained from another by discarding indices, we prefer to connect them not by the equality $=$, but by the isomorphism sign $\cong$.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: In our consideration, we do not use the choice of basis anywhere, so all indices can be understood as abstract.
elements.sent-2411-011: But, if it is more convenient for the reader, one can easily understand them in the usual sense as components, relative to the chosen basis.
elements.sent-2411-012: Each component of the gauge field---vector potential $\bm{A _a \cong A_{a\beta { ^\alpha$ takes values in the corresponding Lie algebra.}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: In the considered below case of $U(n)$ symmetry this will be Lie algebra $\mathfrak{u (n)$ of Hermitian $(n\times n)$-matrices\footnote{Or anti-Hermitian---it's just a matter of convention.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: It is known that in the complex case, Hermitian and anti-Hermitian matrices differ only by an imaginary unit $i$.
elements.sent-2411-015: Among physicists, it is customary to write $i$ in the formula \eqref{CovDerYM , and for them the potential $\bm{A _a$ is Hermitian, mathematicians do not write it, and for them it is anti-Hermitian.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: In EG, a similar expression has the form \begin{equation}\label{SpacetimeCovDer} \nabla_a v^b = \partial_a v^b + \Gamma_{ac}{}^b v^c,\end{equation} where $\Gamma_{ac { ^b$ are the Christoffel symbols.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Then the Yang-Mills field strength $\bm{F _{ab \cong F_{ab\alpha { ^\beta$ and the Riemann tensor $R_{abc { ^d$ are similarly constructed from the potentials $\bm{A _a$ and the Christoffel symbols $\Gamma_{ac { ^b$, respectively \begin{equation}F_{ab} = \partial_a A_b - \partial_b A_a - ie\left[A_a, A_b\right], \label{BundleCurv} R_{abc}{}^d = \partial_a \Gamma_{bc}{}^d - \partial_b \Gamma_{ac}{}^d + \Gamma_{ah}{}^d \Gamma_{bc}{}^h - \Gamma_{bh}{}^d \Gamma_{ac}{}^h. \label{SpactimeCurv}\end{equation} However, immediately after this similarity comes a striking difference: in YM the dynamic field carrying the degrees of freedom is the connection (i.e. the potentials $\bm{A _a$), and in the standard EG the dynamic field is not the affine connection (i.e. the Christoffel symbols $\Gamma_{ac { ^b$), but the metric $g_{ab}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The connection is the usual Levi-Civita connection of Riemannian geometry, fixed by the conditions of torsionlessness and covariant constancy of the metric \begin{equation}\label{CovConstMetric} \nabla_a g_{bc} = 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-019: On a practical level, this is expressed by the fact that the Yang-Mills equations are obtained by varying the Yang-Mills action \begin{equation}S_\mathrm{YM} = -\frac{1}{4} \int d^dx\, \sqrt{g} \tr\left(\bm{F}_{ab}\bm{F}^{ab}\right)\end{equation} with respect to the potentials $\bm{A _a$, while the Einstein equations are obtained by varying the Hilbert-Einstein action \begin{equation}\label{HEaction} S_\mathrm{HE} = M_P^2 \int d^dx\, \sqrt{g}\, R\end{equation} with respect to the metric $g_{ab}$\footnote{Here $g = |\det g_{ab |$, the signature is ``mostly-pluses'' $(-,+,\cdots,+)$, $R = g^{ab R_{ab}$, $R_{ab = R_{acb { ^c$.. .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: It seems quite natural that in order to bring both theories even closer to each other, it is necessary to consider the connection and the metric as independent variables.
elements.sent-2411-021: It is well known that for the Hilbert-Einstein action \eqref{HEaction the so-called ``Palatini approach'' does not lead to anything new, since varying $S_\mathrm{HE}$ with respect to $\Gamma_{ac { ^b$ again leads to the covariant constancy condition \eqref{CovConstMetric (\cite{MThWh , p.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, for other, more complicated gravitational actions (or even simply in the presence of matter fields) this is no longer the case: abandoning the condition \eqref{CovConstMetric takes us beyond the standard Riemannian geometry, and we get some new theory, which is called metric-affine gravity (MAG) \cite{Hehl1976, Buchbinder1992, Hehl1995, PBO2017, Baldazzi2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Note that since the geometry in this case is determined by a larger number of structures, there are more possibilities for constructing an action of such a theory, different from the standard action $S_\mathrm{HE}$.
elements.sent-2411-024: And, although MAG has been studied for decades, it can by no means be said that these models are fully understood.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, our attention is now drawn to a completely different circumstance, which has always remained in shadows before.
elements.sent-2411-026: It is surprising that, as far as we know, no one has previously attempted to construct and study an analogous (and even much simpler) ``metric-affine-like'' generalization of the Yang-Mills theory (mal-YM).
elements.sent-2411-027: In this letter we show how such generalization can be constructed and point out some interesting results that can be obtained this way.
elements.sent-2411-028: To construct the aforementioned generalization of YM, we must first ask the following simple question: if in MAG the partner of the affine connection $\Gamma_{ac { ^b$ is the spacetime metric $g_{ab}$, then what structure must be the partner of the potential $\bm{A _a$ in mal-YM? Perhaps the whole point is simply that for YM such a partner does not exist, and therefore the generalization we seek is impossible? No, this is not so.}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: As is well explained, for example, in the book by Penrose and Rindler (\cite{PenroseRindler1 , pp. 343-344), any specific symmetry in YM is equivalent to the presence of a certain additional structure in its internal color space (i.e. in the fibers of the corresponding vector bundle over spacetime).}
elements.sent-2411-030: Unfortunately, the notation that is convenient for standard YM, and therefore has become widespread in it, leaves these structures in shadows.
elements.sent-2411-031: This is probably the reason why a very simple generalization under consideration has not been discovered earlier.
elements.sent-2411-032: To build it, we need to bring the structure in fibers to the foreground, and for this we have to use a rather unusual Penrose notation \cite{PenroseRindler1 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Since now we want, first of all, to show our general idea, for the sake of simplicity and clarity we will consider here and everywhere below one specific case---a theory with $U(n)$ symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-034: Generalizations to other symmetry groups are straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-035: Also, for simplicity, we will assume that spacetime is flat everywhere below.
elements.sent-2411-036: So, what additional structure in fibers corresponds to $U(n)$ symmetry? In this case, the $n$-dimensional internal color space $V$ (a typical fiber of the bundle) will be complex.
elements.sent-2411-037: Note that the operation of complex conjugation cannot map the color space $V$ into itself, since otherwise it would contain real and imaginary vectors, but all vectors in the color space (on which the fundamental representation of the corresponding group acts) are equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-038: Therefore, complex conjugation will antilinearly map the color space into another space $\bar V$, complex conjugate to it.
elements.sent-2411-039: Such complex conjugate spaces $V$ and $\bar V$ always occur in pairs.
elements.sent-2411-040: We will (following Penrose) denote the indices associated with them by unprimed and primed Greek indices\footnote{The undotted and dotted indices, standardly used to denote Weyl 2-spinors, have the same nature.. .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Since unprimed and primed indices belong to different spaces $V$ and $\bar V$, they cannot be contracted with each other.
elements.sent-2411-042: However, complex conjugation turns primed indices into unprimed ones and vice versa: \begin{equation}\overline{H_{\alpha\ldots\beta'\ldots}^{\gamma\ldots\delta'\ldots}} = \bar H_{\alpha'\ldots\beta\ldots}^{\gamma'\ldots\delta\ldots}\end{equation} Now, suppose we are given a $U(n)$-charged scalar $\bm{\varphi \cong \varphi^\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: How can we construct a covariant real Lagrangian density for it? Obviously, this requires not only the complex conjugate scalar $\bar\varphi^{\alpha'}$, but also a form in the fibers $g_{\alpha\beta'}$, which is Hermitian and non-degenerate \begin{equation}\label{gDef} \bar g_{\alpha\beta'} = g_{\alpha\beta'}, \qquad g_{\alpha\gamma'}g^{\beta\gamma'} = \delta_\alpha^\beta.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: Then, using it, the desired Lagrangian density can be written as \begin{equation}\label{LagPhi} \calL_\varphi = -\frac{1}{2} g_{\alpha\beta'} \nabla_a\varphi^\alpha \nabla^a \bar\varphi^{\beta'} - P\left(|\varphi|^2\right),\end{equation} where $|\varphi|^2 = g_{\alpha\beta'}\varphi^\alpha \bar\varphi^{\beta'}$, and $P\left(|\varphi|^2\right)$ is some lower-bounded potential which describes self-interaction of the field $\bm{\varphi}$.
elements.sent-2411-045: We claim that this Hermitian form in fibers $g_{\alpha\beta'}$ is the very analogue of the spacetime metric $g_{ab}$, which will play the role of potential partner in mal-YM.
elements.sent-2411-046: Indeed, $g_{\alpha\beta'}$ is quite similar in its properties to $g_{ab}$: Hermiticity is an analogue of the symmetry of the metric, and non-degeneracy allows raising and lowering color indices in the usual way.
elements.sent-2411-047: In this case, when changing the position, the unprimed index becomes primed and vice versa, for example, $\bar\varphi_\alpha = g_{\alpha\alpha'}\bar\varphi^{\alpha'}$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Thus, the Hermitian form $g_{\alpha\beta'}$ establishes a (non-canonical) isomorphism $\bar V \cong V^*$ between the complex conjugate and the dual spaces.
elements.sent-2411-049: Moreover, the analogy between YM and EG can be carried much further: in fact, the standard YM also has an implicit assumption that the Hermitian form in the fibers is covariantly constant \begin{equation}\label{CovConstYM} \nabla_a g_{\alpha\beta'} = 0,\end{equation} which is completely analogous to the condition of covariant constancy of the spacetime metric \eqref{CovConstMetric in EG.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Accordingly, the ``metric-affine-like'' generazization of YM can be obtained by dropping this condition.
elements.sent-2411-051: In the remainder of this letter we briefly, without any detailed explanations, list the main consequences of this generalization.
elements.sent-2411-052: All the statements made below will be substantiated in detail in a separate article \cite{Wachowski24b .}
elements.section-2411-003: Hermitian and anti-Hermitian parts and gauge transformations
elements.sent-2411-053: Let us agree to call ``matrices'' linear maps of the internal color space $V\to V$, i.e. tensors with two unprimed indices---one upper and one lower $\bm{M \cong M_\alpha^\beta$, so that the product of matrices is simply a contraction over one of the indices $\bm{M}\bm{N \cong M^\alpha_\gamma N^\gamma_\beta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Let us note the following: the complex conjugate object $\bar M_{\alpha' ^{\beta'}$ is no longer a ``matrix'' in this sense---it is a map of the complex conjugate space $\bar V\to \bar V$, it has primed indices, and therefore cannot be multiplied by ``matrices''.}
elements.sent-2411-055: However, we can define the operation of Hermitian conjugation, which maps matrices to matrices, but contains not only complex conjugation, but also ``transposition,'' i.e. contractions with $g_{\alpha\alpha'}$ and $g^{\beta\beta'}$: \begin{equation}\label{HermConjDef} \bm{M}^\dag \cong \bar M_\alpha^\beta = g_{\alpha\alpha'} g^{\beta\beta'} \bar M_{\beta'}^{\alpha'}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-056: Although it is not explicitly stated in standard algebra courses, Hermitian conjugation always requires the specification of some Hermitian form $g_{\alpha\beta'}$.

elements.sent-2411-061: Using the Hermitian conjugation \eqref{HermConjDef , we can split $\calA_a$ and $\calF_{ab}$ into Hermitian and anti-Hermitian parts \begin{equation}\calA_a = \tilde e\bm{B}_a - ie \bm{A}_a, \qquad \calF_{ab} = \tilde e\bm{G}_{ab} - ie \bm{F}_{ab},\end{equation} where $e$ and $\tilde e$ are two positive coupling constants, and \begin{equation}B_a = \frac{1}{2\tilde e}(\calA_a + \calA_a^\dag), A_a = \frac{i}{2e}(\calA_a - \calA_a^\dag), \label{TotalPotential} G_{ab} = \frac{1}{2\tilde e}(\calF_{ab} + \calF_{ab}^\dag), F_{ab} = \frac{i}{2e}(\calF_{ab} - \calF_{ab}^\dag). \label{TotalCurvature}\end{equation} As we mentioned at the very beginning, in YM only anti-Hermitian components $\bm{F _{ab}$ and $\bm{A _a$ are present.}}}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\calA_a = \tilde eB_a - ie A_a, \qquad \calF_{ab} = \tilde eG_{ab} - ie F_{ab},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-062: Since this statement always appears at the very beginning and is not substantiated in any way, it may seem that this is some kind of axiom.
elements.sent-2411-063: But why should we adhere to it? In fact, this is not an axiom at all, but a very simple consequence of a deeper condition of covariant constancy of the structure in fibers \eqref{CovConstYM .}
elements.sent-2411-064: However, if we abandon this condition, it will no longer be the case.
elements.sent-2411-065: Let us introduce the following quantity, which is a measure of the condition \eqref{CovConstYM violation and which we will therefore call the YM-deviation vector: \begin{equation}\label{YMdeviationDef} \bm{N}_a \cong N_{a\alpha}{}^\beta = -\frac{1}{2\tilde e} g^{\beta\beta'} \nabla_a g_{\alpha\beta'}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-066: It can be shown that the Hermitian part of the total curvature $\bm{G _{ab}$ is completely determined by the YM-deviation vector $\bm{N _a$: \begin{equation}\label{NtoG} \bm{G}_{ab} = \nabla_a \bm{N}_b - \nabla_b \bm{N}_a - 2\tilde e [\bm{N}_a, \bm{N}_b].\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: If the condition \eqref{CovConstYM is satisfied, then the YM-deviation vector vanishes $\bm{N _a = 0$, so $\bm{G _{ab = 0$, and the total curvature in the bundle is reduced to the usual strength tensor $\calF_{ab = -ie \bm{F _{ab}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Thus, we restore the standard YM.
elements.sent-2411-069: Another way to formulate this is as follows: if a connection respects the Hermitian form in fibers, then its total curvature will be anti-Hermitian.
elements.sent-2411-070: There, if we abandon the condition of covariant constancy of the metric \eqref{CovConstMetric , the spacetime curvature ceases to be anti-symmetric with respect to the second pair of indices, but its symmetric part $R_{ab(cd)}$ is completely determined by the non-metricity $\nabla_a g_{cd}$ and vanishes together with it, which returns us to standard EG.) elements.sent-2411-072: However, introducing the Hermitian form $g_{ \alpha\beta'}$ breaks this symmetry to $U(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-073: Indeed, the requirement that $g_{\alpha\beta'}$ must be invariant under transformations \eqref{PureGaugePotencalEq singles out a narrower class of unitary transformations for which $ \bm{U = \bm{u ^\dag$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: If we consider infinitesimal transformations $\bm{u = \mathbf{1 + \bm{\epsilon}$ and split their parameter into Hermitian and anti-Hermitian parts \begin{equation}\bm{\epsilon} = \tilde e\bm{\beta} - ie \bm{\alpha}, \quad \bm{\beta} = \frac{1}{2\tilde e}(\bm{\epsilon} + \bm{\epsilon}^\dag), \quad \bm{\alpha} = \frac{i}{2e}(\bm{\epsilon} - \bm{\epsilon}^\dag),\end{equation} then we have the following transformation of the Hermitian form: \begin{equation}\label{Nonunitary} \tilde g_{\alpha\beta'} = (\delta_\alpha^\beta + h_\alpha^\beta)g_{\beta\beta'}, \quad\text{where}\quad \bm{h} = 2\tilde e\bm{\beta}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Thus, $\bm{\alpha}$ corresponds to usual unitary transformations preserving the Hermitian form $g_{\alpha\beta'}$, and $\bm{\beta $ corresponds to non-unitary transformations that change it.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Therefore, $g_{\alpha\beta'}$ is a Higgs field, and its variation $\bm{h}$ is a Goldstone boson.
elements.sent-2411-077: However, it is well known that due to broken gauge symmetry, Goldstone bosons can be completely eliminated from the theory by redefining other fields.
elements.sent-2411-078: This is the case in mal-YM: according to \eqref{Nonunitary , for any transformation of the Hermitian form $\bm{h}$ we can choose a non-unitary transformation that will reproduce it exactly.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Subtracting this gauge transformation from the changes in connection and charged matter fields, when they are present, we can completely eliminate $\bm{h}$ from the variation of the action.
elements.sent-2411-080: Thus, a perturbation of the Hermitian form $\bm{h}$ is not a physical field carrying degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-081: This also manifests itself in the Noether identities, which relate the sources of classical fields in theories with gauge symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-082: We define anti-Hermitian $\bm{J _a$ and Hermitian $\bm{\Lambda _a$ gauge currents as variations of the action of the theory $S[\nabla, g]$ with respect to the anti-Hermitian $\bm{A _a$ and Hermitian $\bm{B _a$ potentials, respectively, and we also define the Hermitian matrix $\bm{E}$, which is the source of the field $\bm{h}$ \begin{equation}\bm{J}_a = \frac{\delta S}{\delta\bm{A}^a}, \quad \bm{\Lambda}_a = -\frac{\delta S}{\delta\bm{B}^a}, \quad \bm{E} = -2\frac{\delta S}{\delta\bm{h}}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Then in pure mal-YM without matter fields, the Noether identities following from the symmetry under unitary $\bm{\alpha}$ and non-unutary $\bm{\beta}$ gauge transformations, respectively, will be of the form: \begin{equation}\nabla_aJ^a - \tilde e[N_a, J^a] + ie [N_a, \Lambda^a] = 0, \nabla_a\Lambda^a - \tilde e[N_a, \Lambda^a] - i\frac{\tilde e^2}{e} [N_a, J^a] = \tilde eE.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation}\nabla_aJ^a - \tilde e[N_a, J^a] + ie [N_a, \Lambda^a] = 0, \nabla_a\Lambda^a - \tilde e[N_a, \Lambda^a] - i\frac{\tilde e^2}{e} [N_a, J^a] = \tilde eE.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-084: The first of these relations is a modification of the conservation law of the Yang-Mills current, which curiously ceases to be preserved in mal-YM.
elements.sent-2411-085: And the second relation means that the equation obtained by varying the action with respect to $\bm{h}$ is not an independent EoM, but only a differential consequence of other EoMs.
elements.sent-2411-086: Summarizing the above, now in our theory in addition to the usual Yang-Mills field $\bm{F _{ab}$, $\bm{A _a$ there appears a second dynamical gauge field $\bm{G _{ab}$, $\bm{B _a$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The dynamics of these two fields and their interactions with each other are determined by the form of the action.

elements.sent-2411-090: The introduction of the third mass term is motivated by the natural desire to be able to restore the condition \eqref{CovConstYM and, consequently, the standard YM in limit $M\to\infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Of course, we could also introduce other terms, for example, $\tr(\bm{G _{ab}\bm{F ^{ab )$, $\tr([\bm{N _a, \bm{N _b]\bm{F ^{ab )$, etc., but we do not do this solely for the sake of simplicity, in order not to clutter the presentation.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The Lagrangian \eqref{totalLagrangian leads to the following classical equations on the background fields: \begin{equation}\nabla^b F_{ab} - \tilde e [N^b, F_{ab}] - ie [N^b, G_{ab}] = J_a^ext, \label{EqAfinal} \nabla^b G_{ab} - \tilde e [N^b, G_{ab}] + i\frac{\tilde e^2}{e} [N^b, F_{ab}] + M^2N_a = -\Lambda_a^ext, \label{EqBfinal}\end{equation} where on the right-hand side we have added external anti-Hermitian $\bm{J _a^\mathrm{ext}$ and Hermitian $\bm{\Lambda _a^\mathrm{ext}$ gauge currents that can be generated by the matter fields if they are present in the theory.}}}
elements.formula-2411-024: \begin{equation}\nabla^b F_{ab} - \tilde e [N^b, F_{ab}] - ie [N^b, G_{ab}] = J_a^ext, \label{EqAfinal} \nabla^b G_{ab} - \tilde e [N^b, G_{ab}] + i\frac{\tilde e^2}{e} [N^b, F_{ab}] + M^2N_a = -\Lambda_a^ext, \label{EqBfinal}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-094: It is easy to see that the first equation is the usual equation for a massless gauge field (with the degenerate wave operator, which requires a subsequent gauge fixing procedure), and the second equation is the Proca equation for a vector field of mass $M$, equivalent to the equation $(\Box + M^2) \bm{B _a = 0$ with the additional condition $\nabla^a\bm{B _a = 0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: It can be noted that if we look for solutions of the equations \eqref{EqAfinal -\eqref{EqBfinal without external sources, for which $\bm{N _a = 0$ (and hence also $\bm{G _{ab = 0$), then the remaining field $\bm{F _{ab}$ will satisfy the usual Yang-Mills equation $\nabla^b\bm{F _{ab = 0$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Thus, any classical solution of pure YM is at the same time a solution of pure mal-YM.
elements.sent-2411-097: However, it apparently follows from the equation \eqref{LinEqB that, at least in the vicinity of the trivial background, mal-YM has solutions that are not borrowed from YM.}
elements.sent-2411-098: It also follows from the equation \eqref{EqBfinal that when the mass of the second field tends to infinity, $M\to\infty$, we obtain $\bm{N _a = 0$, i.e. we restore the covariant constancy condition \eqref{CovConstYM and return to the standard YM.}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: To summarize, we have constructed what seems to be a very natural generalization of YM, in which in addition to the usual massless Yang-Mills gauge fields $\bm{A _a$ and $\bm{F _{ab}$, there appear some new non-trivially interacting fields $\bm{B _a$ and $\bm{G _{ab}$, which can be made massive.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Letting their mass tend to infinity $M\to\infty$ restores the standard YM.
elements.sent-2411-101: Here we have considered this theory exclusively at the classical level.
elements.sent-2411-102: Although at first glance it seems that it is renormalizable and unitary, this, as well as the absence of other pathologies, requires a separate careful check.
elements.sent-2411-103: If it turns out that such a theory for some reason cannot be considered physical, this will already be an interesting result, since it will allow us to understand the nature of the covariant constancy condition \eqref{CovConstYM , which underlies the usual YM.}
elements.sent-2411-104: However, if mal-YM turns out to be a physically admissible theory, then such a new generalization with additional parameters has great phenomenological interest.
elements.sent-2411-105: Indeed, the fact that we do not observe the fields $\bm{B _a$ and $\bm{G _{ab}$ can always be explained by the too large mass $M$.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: However, this immediately leads us to the question of what is the lower bound to this mass compatible with the data of accelerator experiments and observational cosmology? Further, can the new theory help explain some phenomena? And can it be that some of the periodically raising (and usually dissolving) deviations from the Standard Model are better described by the new theory at some values of its parameters $e$, $\tilde e$ and $M$? In addition to these applied questions, it seems to us that mal-YM also has a more abstract theoretical significance, allowing us to take a new look at the metric-affine gravity (MAG).
elements.sent-2411-107: Typically the standard Hilbert-Einstein action \eqref{HEaction is considered.}
elements.sent-2411-108: However, our considerations of the mal-YM above also hint that one should probably consider an action quadratic in the curvatures, which is renormalizable and at the same time, in MAG, will not lead to equations with higher derivatives.
